title: Bayes' Theorem
assumptions: A and B are events in a probability space (\Omega, \mathcal{F}, P)
intuition: Bayes' theorem lets us reverse conditional probabilities — if we know P(B|A), we can find P(A|B). It is the foundation of Bayesian inference.
pitfalls: P(B) must be strictly positive; conditioning on a zero-probability event is undefined.
Bayes' theorem does not imply causation between A and B.
references: Bayes, T. (1763). An essay towards solving a problem in the doctrine of chances.
Kolmogorov, A. N. (1933). Grundbegriffe der Wahrscheinlichkeitsrechnung.

derivationSteps.explanation: By definition, P(A|B) = P(A∩B)/P(B), so rearranging gives P(A∩B) = P(A|B)·P(B).
hints: The conditional probability P(A|B) represents the probability of A given that B has occurred.

derivationSteps.explanation: Since A∩B = B∩A, applying the same definition with roles of A and B swapped gives P(B∩A) = P(B|A)·P(A).
hints: Intersection is commutative: A∩B and B∩A describe the same event.

derivationSteps.expression: P(A \mid B)\, P(B) = P(B \mid A)\, P(A)
derivationSteps.explanation: Steps 1 and 2 both equal P(A∩B), so the right-hand sides are equal.
Dividing both sides of step 3 by P(B) > 0 isolates P(A|B). This is Bayes' theorem.
hints: We can divide by P(B) because it is strictly positive by assumption.

examples.title: Medical Test Example
examples.setup: A disease has 1% prevalence. A test is 99% sensitive (P(+|D)=0.99) and 95% specific (P(-|\bar{D})=0.95). Given a positive test, what is P(D|+)?
examples.solution: P(D|+) = (0.99 × 0.01) / (0.99×0.01 + 0.05×0.99) ≈ 0.167. Despite a positive test, only ~17% chance of disease — the base rate matters enormously.

title: Law of Total Probability
assumptions: \{A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_n\} is a partition of \Omega
intuition: To compute P(B) when it is hard to measure directly, we condition on a partition of the sample space and sum up the weighted conditional probabilities.
pitfalls: The partition must be exhaustive and mutually exclusive.
references: Feller, W. (1968). An Introduction to Probability Theory and Its Applications. Vol. 1.

derivationSteps.explanation: Since {A_i} partitions Ω, their union equals Ω. Intersecting B with Ω leaves B unchanged.
Intersection distributes over union: B ∩ (∪ A_i) = ∪ (B ∩ A_i).
The sets B∩A_i are mutually disjoint (since the A_i partition Ω), so countable additivity of probability gives P(∪(B∩A_i)) = Σ P(B∩A_i).
hints: Mutual disjointness follows from the partition property of {A_i}.

derivationSteps.explanation: Applying P(B∩A_i) = P(B|A_i)·P(A_i) to each term completes the proof.

title: Linearity of Expectation
assumptions: X and Y are random variables with finite expectations
intuition: The expected value of a linear combination is the linear combination of expected values — no independence assumption is needed.
pitfalls: Linearity holds regardless of dependence between X and Y.
references: Durrett, R. (2019). Probability: Theory and Examples. 5th ed.

derivationSteps.expression: \mathbb{E}[aX + bY] = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} (ax + by)\, f_{X,Y}(x, y)\, dx\, dy
derivationSteps.explanation: For jointly continuous random variables, expectation is defined as the double integral of the value times the joint density.
assumptions: X, Y jointly continuous with density f_{X,Y}

derivationSteps.expression: = \int\int ax\, f_{X,Y}\, dx\, dy + \int\int by\, f_{X,Y}\, dx\, dy
derivationSteps.explanation: Splitting the integrand (ax + by) into two terms and separating the integrals (valid by Fubini's theorem given finite expectations).

derivationSteps.expression: = a\int x\left(\int f_{X,Y}\, dy\right) dx + b\int y\left(\int f_{X,Y}\, dx\right) dy
derivationSteps.explanation: Constants a and b factor out. Integrating f_{X,Y} over one variable yields the marginal density of the other.
hints: ∫f_{X,Y}(x,y) dy = f_X(x) (the marginal density of X)

derivationSteps.expression: = a\int x\, f_X(x)\, dx + b\int y\, f_Y(y)\, dy
derivationSteps.explanation: Integrating the joint density over y gives the marginal density of X, and vice versa.
Each remaining integral is precisely the definition of E[X] and E[Y] respectively.
